text: This chapter is different in style from the other chapters of the book. The facts here are famous theorems, and their proofs require one-off, clever ideas. These particular ideas will not turn up again on homework or exams. Think of this chapter instead as a capstone: we explore what kinds of mathematical statements are in reach with the reasoning tools and theory we have developed so far in the book.
html: This chapter is different in style from the other chapters of the book. The facts here are famous theorems, and their proofs require one-off, clever ideas. These particular ideas will not turn up again on homework or exams. Think of this chapter instead as a capstone: we explore what kinds of mathematical statements are in reach with the reasoning tools and theory we have developed so far in the book.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: There are infinitely many prime numbers.
html: There are infinitely many prime numbers.

text: This is a an extremely old theorem, with the first known proof written in Euclid’s Elements in about 300 BC.
html: This is a an extremely old theorem, with the first known proof written in Euclid’s Elements in about 300 BC.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We show that for a given natural number \(N\) , there exists a prime number \(p \geq N\) .
html: We show that for a given natural number \(N\), there exists a prime number \(p \geq N\).

text: Consider \(N!\) , the factorial of \(N\) . By one of the exercises to Section 6.2 , \(0<N!\) , so \(2 \le N! + 1\) . Therefore, by Example 6.4.2 , there exists a prime number \(p\) which is a factor of \(N! + 1\) .
html: Consider \(N!\), the factorial of \(N\). By one of the exercises to Section 6.2, \(0<N!\), so \(2 \le N! + 1\). Therefore, by Example 6.4.2, there exists a prime number \(p\) which is a factor of \(N! + 1\).

text: Let \(k\) be a natural number such that \(N! + 1=pk\) . This number \(k\) cannot be zero, since if it were, we would have
html: Let \(k\) be a natural number such that \(N! + 1=pk\). This number \(k\) cannot be zero, since if it were, we would have

text: contradiction. Thus \(k>0\) , so \(k\) is of the form \(l+1\) for some natural number \(l\) , and \(N! + 1=p(l+1)\) .
html: contradiction. Thus \(k>0\), so \(k\) is of the form \(l+1\) for some natural number \(l\), and \(N! + 1=p(l+1)\).

text: We now show that \(p\) is not a factor of \(N!\) , by showing that \(N!\) lies between the consecutive multiples \(pl\) and \(p(l+1)\) of \(p\) . (This is the test from Example 4.5.8 .) Indeed,
html: We now show that \(p\) is not a factor of \(N!\), by showing that \(N!\) lies between the consecutive multiples \(pl\) and \(p(l+1)\) of \(p\). (This is the test from Example 4.5.8.) Indeed,

text: so \(pl< N!\) , and
html: so \(pl< N!\), and

text: If \(p\le N\) , then by Example 6.2.5 , \(p\) would be a factor of \(N!\) , which contradicts what we just proved. Therefore \(p > N\) . This gives the required prime number greater than or equal to \(N\) .
html: If \(p\le N\), then by Example 6.2.5, \(p\) would be a factor of \(N!\), which contradicts what we just proved. Therefore \(p > N\). This gives the required prime number greater than or equal to \(N\).

text: In Lean, the exercise to Section 6.2 has the name factorial_pos , Example 6.4.2 has the name exists_prime_factor , Example 4.5.8 has the name Nat.not_dvd_of_exists_lt_and_lt , and Example 6.2.5 has the name dvd_factorial .
html: In Lean, the exercise to Section 6.2 has the name factorial_pos, Example 6.4.2 has the name exists_prime_factor, Example 4.5.8 has the name Nat.not_dvd_of_exists_lt_and_lt, and Example 6.2.5 has the name dvd_factorial.

text: Theorem (Gauss’ lemma)
html: Theorem (Gauss’ lemma)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(d\) be integers. Suppose that \(ab\) is a multiple of \(d\) and \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,d)=1\) . Then \(b\) is a multiple of \(d\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(d\) be integers. Suppose that \(ab\) is a multiple of \(d\) and \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,d)=1\). Then \(b\) is a multiple of \(d\).

text: This lemma is the “final form” of the argument we have carried out in special cases in Example 3.5.1 , Example 3.5.2 , etc. Like in those special cases, the trick is to find a “Bézout identity” relating \(a\) and \(d\) : multiples of \(a\) and \(d\) which differ by 1. In the special cases we could find such multiples explicitly. In the general situation the existence of such multiples is guaranteed by Example 6.7.6 .
html: This lemma is the “final form” of the argument we have carried out in special cases in Example 3.5.1, Example 3.5.2, etc. Like in those special cases, the trick is to find a “Bézout identity” relating \(a\) and \(d\): multiples of \(a\) and \(d\) which differ by 1. In the special cases we could find such multiples explicitly. In the general situation the existence of such multiples is guaranteed by Example 6.7.6.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: By Example 6.7.6 (Bézout’s identity) there exist integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that \(xa + yd = \operatorname{gcd}(a, d)\) . Since \(ab\) is a multiple of \(d\) , there exists an integer \(z\) such that \(ab=dz\) . We then have that
html: By Example 6.7.6 (Bézout’s identity) there exist integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that \(xa + yd = \operatorname{gcd}(a, d)\). Since \(ab\) is a multiple of \(d\), there exists an integer \(z\) such that \(ab=dz\). We then have that

text: so \(b\) is a multiple of \(d\) .
html: so \(b\) is a multiple of \(d\).

text: Theorem (Euclid’s lemma)
html: Theorem (Euclid’s lemma)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(p\) be natural numbers, with \(p\) prime. Suppose that \(ab\) is a multiple of \(p\) . Then either \(a\) or \(b\) is a multiple of \(p\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(p\) be natural numbers, with \(p\) prime. Suppose that \(ab\) is a multiple of \(p\). Then either \(a\) or \(b\) is a multiple of \(p\).

text: This lemma also dates back to Euclid’s Elements .
html: This lemma also dates back to Euclid’s Elements.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: By Example 6.7.2 , \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,p)\geq 0\) , so \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,p)\) (a priori an integer) can be considered as a natural number. Let us call that natural number \(d\) . Then
html: By Example 6.7.2, \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,p)\geq 0\), so \(\operatorname{gcd}(a,p)\) (a priori an integer) can be considered as a natural number. Let us call that natural number \(d\). Then

text: A priori these divisibility statements are all statements about \(a\) , \(b\) , \(p\) and \(d\) considered as integers, but these are equivalent to the corresponding divisibility statements about natural numbers.
html: A priori these divisibility statements are all statements about \(a\), \(b\), \(p\) and \(d\) considered as integers, but these are equivalent to the corresponding divisibility statements about natural numbers.

text: We now begin the proof proper. Since \(p\) is prime and \(d\mid p\) , either \(d=1\) or \(d=p\) .
html: We now begin the proof proper. Since \(p\) is prime and \(d\mid p\), either \(d=1\) or \(d=p\).

text: Case 1 ( \(d=1\) ): Then by ( \(\star\) ), \(p \mid b\) .
html: Case 1 (\(d=1\)): Then by (\(\star\)), \(p \mid b\).

text: Case 2 ( \(d=p\) ): Then since \(d\mid a\) , \(p\mid a\) .
html: Case 2 (\(d=p\)): Then since \(d\mid a\), \(p\mid a\).

text: To write this proof in Lean, we need some tricks to handle the interaction between integers and natural numbers. These will not be used again and it’s fine not to read them very closely.
html: To write this proof in Lean, we need some tricks to handle the interaction between integers and natural numbers. These will not be used again and it’s fine not to read them very closely.

text: Corollary
html: Corollary

text: Let \(a\) , \(p\) and \(k\) be natural numbers, with \(p\) prime and \(k\geq 1\) . If \(a^k\) is a multiple of \(p\) , then \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(p\) and \(k\) be natural numbers, with \(p\) prime and \(k\geq 1\). If \(a^k\) is a multiple of \(p\), then \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We prove this by induction on \(k\) , starting at 1.
html: We prove this by induction on \(k\), starting at 1.

text: Base case: If \(a^1\) is a multiple of \(p\) , then since \(a^1=a\) , we conclude that \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\) .
html: Base case: If \(a^1\) is a multiple of \(p\), then since \(a^1=a\), we conclude that \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\).

text: Inductive step: Let \(t\) be a natural number and suppose that if \(a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\) then \(a\) is. ( \(\star\) )
html: Inductive step: Let \(t\) be a natural number and suppose that if \(a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\) then \(a\) is. (\(\star\))

text: Now suppose that \(a^{t+1}\) is a multiple of \(p\) . Since \(a^{t+1}=a\cdot a^t\) , this means that \(a \cdot a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\) .
html: Now suppose that \(a^{t+1}\) is a multiple of \(p\). Since \(a^{t+1}=a\cdot a^t\), this means that \(a \cdot a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\).

text: Therefore, by Euclid’s lemma, either \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\) , in which case we are done, or \(a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\) , in which case by the inductive hypothesis ( \(\star\) ) we are done.
html: Therefore, by Euclid’s lemma, either \(a\) is a multiple of \(p\), in which case we are done, or \(a^t\) is a multiple of \(p\), in which case by the inductive hypothesis (\(\star\)) we are done.

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: There do not exist natural numbers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\) , for which \(a^2=2b^2\) .
html: There do not exist natural numbers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\), for which \(a^2=2b^2\).

text: This is another theorem which dates back to the ancient Greeks, in this case to the followers of Pythagoras , c. 450 BC.
html: This is another theorem which dates back to the ancient Greeks, in this case to the followers of Pythagoras, c. 450 BC.

text: This theorem constitutes most of the work of proving that the square root of two is irrational , that is, not a rational number ( \(\mathbb{Q}\) ). We can’t draw that conclusion here since we haven’t yet defined rational numbers precisely, but we will circle back to it later in the book.
html: This theorem constitutes most of the work of proving that the square root of two is irrational, that is, not a rational number (\(\mathbb{Q}\)). We can’t draw that conclusion here since we haven’t yet defined rational numbers precisely, but we will circle back to it later in the book.

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: We will show a logically equivalent fact: that for all natural numbers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\) , it holds that \(a^2\ne 2b^2\) . We prove this by strong induction on \(b\) .
html: We will show a logically equivalent fact: that for all natural numbers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\), it holds that \(a^2\ne 2b^2\). We prove this by strong induction on \(b\).

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be natural numbers and suppose that for all natural numbers \(r\) , \(s\) with \(s<b\) and \(s\ne 0\) , it holds that \(r^2\ne 2s^2\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be natural numbers and suppose that for all natural numbers \(r\), \(s\) with \(s<b\) and \(s\ne 0\), it holds that \(r^2\ne 2s^2\).

text: Suppose that \(a^2= 2b^2\) . Then \(a^2\) is even, so by one of the exercises to Section 6.1 , \(a\) is even. Let \(k\) be a natural number such that \(a=2k\) . Then
html: Suppose that \(a^2= 2b^2\). Then \(a^2\) is even, so by one of the exercises to Section 6.1, \(a\) is even. Let \(k\) be a natural number such that \(a=2k\). Then

text: so \(b^2=2k^2\) . Then
html: so \(b^2=2k^2\). Then
so \(k>0\), so \(k\ne 0\).

text: We therefore invoke the inductive hypothesis with \(r=b\) , \(s=k\) . (Note that
html: We therefore invoke the inductive hypothesis with \(r=b\), \(s=k\). (Note that

text: so \(k<b\) , so the induction is well-founded.) The inductive hypothesis gives that \(b^2\ne 2k^2\) , contradiction. So it cannot be that \(a^2\ne 2b^2\) .
html: so \(k<b\), so the induction is well-founded.) The inductive hypothesis gives that \(b^2\ne 2k^2\), contradiction. So it cannot be that \(a^2\ne 2b^2\).

text: In Lean we break this argument into three pieces. The lemma irrat_aux_wf is the justification for the well-foundedness of the strong induction. Like in Example 6.7.1 , we tag the well-foundedness lemma with @[decreasing] to make it available later for the strong induction.
html: In Lean we break this argument into three pieces. The lemma irrat_aux_wf is the justification for the well-foundedness of the strong induction. Like in Example 6.7.1, we tag the well-foundedness lemma with @[decreasing] to make it available later for the strong induction.

text: The lemma irrat_aux is the claim which is proved by strong induction. It contains the central argument of the proof. In Lean, the exercise to Section 6.1 which is used in this step has the name Nat.even_of_pow_even .
html: The lemma irrat_aux is the claim which is proved by strong induction. It contains the central argument of the proof. In Lean, the exercise to Section 6.1 which is used in this step has the name Nat.even_of_pow_even.

text: Finally, the main theorem is actually logically equivalent to irrat_aux , and its proof consists of establishing this logical equivalence.
html: Finally, the main theorem is actually logically equivalent to irrat_aux, and its proof consists of establishing this logical equivalence.

text: That was fun … let’s do it again! Here is a different proof of the same theorem. Or, nearly the same theorem – this proof works better for integers. (It would be fiddly but not fundamentally difficult to convert the integer version to the natural number version or vice versa, using the techniques from Section 7.2 .)
html: That was fun … let’s do it again! Here is a different proof of the same theorem. Or, nearly the same theorem – this proof works better for integers. (It would be fiddly but not fundamentally difficult to convert the integer version to the natural number version or vice versa, using the techniques from Section 7.2.)

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: There do not exist integers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\) , for which \(a^2=2b^2\) .
html: There do not exist integers \(a\) and \(b\) with \(b\ne 0\), for which \(a^2=2b^2\).

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: Suppose that \(a\) and \(b\) were integers with \(b\ne 0\) and \(a^2=2b^2\) .
html: Suppose that \(a\) and \(b\) were integers with \(b\ne 0\) and \(a^2=2b^2\).

text: Denote by \(d\) the greatest common divisor of \(a\) and \(b\) . By Example 6.7.3 , \(d\mid a\) and \(d\mid b\) . Let \(k\) and \(l\) be integers such that \(a=dk\) and \(b=dl\) .
html: Denote by \(d\) the greatest common divisor of \(a\) and \(b\). By Example 6.7.3, \(d\mid a\) and \(d\mid b\). Let \(k\) and \(l\) be integers such that \(a=dk\) and \(b=dl\).

text: Also by Example 6.7.6 (Bézout’s identity) there exist integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that \(xa+yb=d\) .
html: Also by Example 6.7.6 (Bézout’s identity) there exist integers \(x\) and \(y\) such that \(xa+yb=d\).

text: The key calculation is the following ( \(\dagger\) ).:
html: The key calculation is the following (\(\dagger\)).:

text: We have that \(d\ne 0\) , since if not,
html: We have that \(d\ne 0\), since if not,

text: contradiction. Hence by ( \(\dagger\) ),
html: contradiction. Hence by (\(\dagger\)),

text: But by Example 2.3.5 , no integer squares to 2, so this is impossible.
html: But by Example 2.3.5, no integer squares to 2, so this is impossible.

text: Note the use of Brahmagupta’s identity ( Example 1.1.3 ) above in the middle of the key calculation.
html: Note the use of Brahmagupta’s identity (Example 1.1.3) above in the middle of the key calculation.

text: by Example 6.7.3 , \(d\mid a\) and \(d\mid p\) ;
html: • by Example 6.7.3, \(d\mid a\) and \(d\mid p\);

text: ( \(\star\) ) by Gauss’ lemma, if \(p\mid ab\) and \(d=1\) then \(p \mid b\) .
html: • (\(\star\)) by Gauss’ lemma, if \(p\mid ab\) and \(d=1\) then \(p \mid b\).

text: When invoking lemmas about integers ( gcd_dvd_left , gcd_dvd_right , gauss_lemma , gcd_nonneg ) on natural number inputs, we cast the natural number inputs to integers, like (a:ℤ) – this is not always necessary but it avoids ambiguity. (The casts then display in the infoview with arrows, as ↑a , etc.)
html: • When invoking lemmas about integers (gcd_dvd_left, gcd_dvd_right, gauss_lemma, gcd_nonneg) on natural number inputs, we cast the natural number inputs to integers, like (a:ℤ) – this is not always necessary but it avoids ambiguity. (The casts then display in the infoview with arrows, as ↑a, etc.)

text: Once we have the hypothesis 0 ≤ gcd (a:ℤ) (p:ℤ) , we can use the Lean tactic lift to introduce a natural number d whose cast to the integers is gcd (a:ℤ) (p:ℤ) .
html: • Once we have the hypothesis 0 ≤ gcd (a:ℤ) (p:ℤ), we can use the Lean tactic lift to introduce a natural number d whose cast to the integers is gcd (a:ℤ) (p:ℤ).

text: Finally, we can run the tactic norm_cast on all our hypotheses, which converts statements about natural-numbers-cast-to-integers into the corresponding statements about natural numbers, if this is mathematically valid. For example, ↑d ∣ ↑a is converted to d ∣ a .
html: • Finally, we can run the tactic norm_cast on all our hypotheses, which converts statements about natural-numbers-cast-to-integers into the corresponding statements about natural numbers, if this is mathematically valid. For example, ↑d ∣ ↑a is converted to d ∣ a.
